title: ABS: Biofuels under the IMO and EU regulatory framework
summary: The EU's updated MRV and ETS regulations now mandate comprehensive reporting for shipping, including methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O) emissions. While certified biofuels can be counted as zero-CO2 for EU ETS compliance, their actual emissions still require reporting with default factors under MRV, creating a questionable dual accounting system. This regulatory inconsistency risks masking true carbon intensity reductions, demanding vigilant scrutiny of all "sustainable" fuel claims.
content:
The EU has updated its maritime emissions rules to include shipping in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS). To support this change, Regulation (EU) 2023/957 and Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2776 have introduced new reporting requirements under the EU MRV system (Monitoring, Reporting and Verification).

These updates require shipping companies to report total greenhouse gas emissions, including specific rules for sustainable biofuels. Normally, a standard CO2 emission factor is used, but biofuels that meet EU sustainability criteria under the Renewable Energy Directive (RED II) may be treated differently.

If a biofuel is certified as sustainable, the CO2 emissions from its biomass part can be counted as zero for EU ETS purposes. However, for EU MRV reporting, default emission factors must still be used when calculating emissions.

The updated rules also require shipping companies to report two additional greenhouse gases: methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O). These are calculated based on how much fuel is used, using standard emission factors provided by the EU. Some biofuels, such as Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil (HVO), already have specific emission factors listed.

If a biofuel is not included in the official list, default values must be used for emissions calculations.

To qualify as sustainable, biofuels must be certified under approved EU sustainability schemes. These include systems such as the International Sustainability and Carbon Certification (ISCC EU) and the Roundtable on Sustainable Biofuels (RSB EU RED), among others listed by the European Commission.
